ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general rice noodle class; no specific entry for thick bee hoon / thick rice vermicelli. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers rice noodles broadly; no sub-code specific to thick-gauge bee hoon. Approximate match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for thick rice vermicelli as a prepared food item.

preparation_method: served in soup or dry with sauce

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical add-on serving of thick bee hoon in Singapore hawker centres is approximately 150 g of cooked noodles.

nutrient_source.title: Thick Bee Hoon (Thick Rice Vermicelli) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Thick Bee Hoon (Thick Rice Vermicelli), cr_id G00010182, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice noodles (analogous estimate)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for thick bee hoon specifically. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on cooked rice noodles (GI ~61, medium) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Thick rice vermicelli shares the same rice-flour base and similar starch gelatinisation profile as standard rice noodles. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (61 × 25.19 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 23.2 for a 150 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g, partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as soy sauce, fish sauce, or salty soup broth, as the dish sodium can escalate well beyond 500 mg per meal when condiments are added.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~61) and 25.2 g carbohydrate/100 g, partially mitigated by 3.7 g dietary fibre/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should restrict servings to 150 g (approximately 38 g available carbohydrate), pair with non-starchy vegetables and a lean protein source to lower the overall glycemic response, and monitor post-meal blood glucose given the moderate-to-high GI of rice-flour noodles.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.5 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and meaningful dietary fibre (3.7 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Thick bee hoon is a low-saturated-fat, cholesterol-free carbohydrate source that is broadly compatible with a heart-healthy diet; however, sodium at 231 mg/100 g warrants attention for individuals on sodium-restricted cardiac diets, and high-fat or high-sodium toppings should be minimised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low saturated fat, zero trans fat, and 3.7 g/100 g dietary fibre supporting gut microbiome
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Thick bee hoon presents minimal pro-inflammatory risk due to its low saturated fat content; however, its refined rice-flour base carries a moderate glycemic load that may contribute to low-grade systemic inflammation if consumed in large portions, so pairing with anti-inflammatory toppings such as ginger or leafy greens is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thick Bee Hoon (粗米粉, Bihun Tebal) is a type of thick rice vermicelli widely consumed in Singapore and Malaysia as a hawker staple. It is made from rice flour and water, extruded into thick round strands and then boiled or blanched until soft and slightly chewy. The texture is notably denser and more substantial than standard thin bee hoon, with a mild, neutral flavour that readily absorbs the taste of accompanying broths, sauces, and toppings. In Singapore hawker culture, thick bee hoon is commonly served as an add-on component in dishes such as fish soup, prawn noodle soup, or dry mixed noodles, and is popular across Malay, Chinese, and Indian-Muslim food stalls. Per 100 g cooked, it provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, and 231 mg sodium. The dietary fibre content is relatively high for a noodle product and helps moderate the glycemic response. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 61 (medium), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium is borderline at 231 mg/100 g but is substantially offset by a favourable potassium level of 327 mg/100 g. The dish is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, vegan, and halal, making it suitable for a wide range of dietary requirements. Clinically, it is appropriate for most adults in moderate portions, with caution advised for those with hypertension or type 2 diabetes when high-sodium sauces or large servings are involved.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010182) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for rice noodles, which is the closest defensible approved GI source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry G00010182 as of 2026-04-16; the HPB record reports only total fat (4.21 g/100 g). vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, and vitamin_e_mg are set to -1: individual vitamin data for this specific thick bee hoon preparation are not reported in HPB Foodo entry G00010182; the HPB database does not publish micronutrient vitamin breakdowns for this dish as of 2026-04-16. phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1: these mineral values are not reported in HPB Foodo entry G00010182 as of 2026-04-16. water_g is set to -1: moisture content is not reported in HPB Foodo entry G00010182 as of 2026-04-16. All -1 sentinel values reflect genuine data gaps in the HPB Singapore Foodo source and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific Singaporean preparation. saturated_fat_g is estimated at 0.5 g/100 g based on the total fat of 4.21 g/100 g and the typical saturated fat fraction (~12%) for rice-flour noodle products per USDA FoodData Central rice noodle entries; this is an approximation and should be verified if precise saturated fat data becomes available from HPB. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB source — plant-based rice noodle product contains no cholesterol. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on cooked rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for thick bee hoon was located in approved sources. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (61 × 25.19 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 23.2.
